Topic 3: Part 1 (the media disconnect & the informal truce)

[101:06]Which is that at long last we finally have the memorandum of understanding between

[101:11]Iran and the United States

[101:14]And there is so much to say about it

[101:18]because like with everything else in this war,

[101:23]it's very ambiguous.

[101:25]And this has characterized the war from the beginning.

[101:28]The way that I would say it is

[101:31]the war is happening in reality.

[101:34]And at the same time,

[101:35]the war is being talked about in press releases

[101:39]and through the media.

[101:41]And what is interesting is that what is being said through press releases and in the media

[101:46]almost has no relation to what is actually happening in reality in the world.

[101:53]And what I mean by that is, how many times have we heard over the past three and a half

[101:58]months that a ceasefire is imminent, that we have these advanced negotiations?

[102:04]How many times has Trump threatened to bomb Iran or annihilate their civilization?

[102:09]There has been so much deception about what exactly we are doing there, the number of casualties,

[102:21]what the strategy is. Everything has been up in the air. There's been a lot of deception

[102:58]So it was announced this weekend that finally, and this we know for sure, there is an agreement.

[103:05]Now they're calling it a memorandum of understanding.

[103:09]This is different from a treaty because a treaty has to be ratified by the legislature.

[103:19]A memorandum of understanding is much more informal.

[103:24]It's more of an agreement in principle without a lot of specifics.

[103:29]And it doesn't govern all of the big issues.

[103:33]So what this really is, the way to think about it is this is a formalization of the ceasefire

Topic 4: Part 2 (how the USA lost)

[107:30]And we'll talk about who's right here.

[107:57]of Hormuz without charge, saying it intended to charge fees, but not tolls.

[108:03]A ceremonial signing of the agreement is to take place on Friday in Geneva.

[108:09]On Sunday, the United States and Iran signed a framework agreement ending their months

[108:12]long war, but a day later, officials on both sides were making conflicting statements about

[108:18]its terms.

[108:19]The agreement's immediate scope was focused primarily on ending military strikes and allowing

[108:29]But, American and Iranian officials acknowledged that they had put off discussions of their

[109:22]is the easing of American economic sanctions against Iran and limiting Tehran's nuclear

[109:40]of the deal, the deal covers in particular, the actual fighting between the United States

[109:47]and Iran. So, we could call that the business of the actual ceasefire.

[109:52]So, what the memorandum of understanding does first and foremost is it ends the war.

[109:57]It says that there's a cessation of all hostilities in the Persian Gulf and then maybe more controversially

[110:04]and more contentiously also in Lebanon.

[110:08]So that's number one.

[110:10]Number two, it governs what happens in the strait.

[110:13]So currently Iran has closed the strait of Hormuz and the United States has blockaded

[110:21]So in addition to ending all hostilities in the Gulf and in Lebanon, the deal also has

[110:26]this mutual opening of the straight, the US ends its blockade and Iran opens up the straight.

[110:32]So you could say that's the business of the straight of Hormuz and its opening.

[110:44]future negotiations on the deeper, more systemic issues,

[110:49]which are the nuclear program and the sanctions.

[110:53]And if you recall, those are actually the reasons

[110:56]the war started in the first place.

[110:58]This is why the US and Israel attacked Iran last year.

[111:02]And ostensibly, this is why we attacked Iran again this year.

[111:06]It was to degrade or potentially destroy Iran's nuclear program, to degrade and destroy their

[111:13]missile arsenal, ultimately to seek regime change.

[111:18]Now we're not even considering regime change, obviously.

[111:21]The Iranian regime will not negotiate its own destruction.

[111:25]Iran has ruled out any discussions about their missile program or their proxies.

[111:31]And so the only issue that we actually got into the war in the first place to solve,

[111:36]which is the nuclear issue, it's not even covered by this memorandum of understanding.

[111:42]The only thing the MOU says about that is that we're going to talk about it later.

[111:46]And so you could say the third thing is the business of future talks.

[111:51]It says that while this ceasefire is extended and while we open up the straight, there will

[111:57]be a 60 day period where we basically negotiate the rest.

[112:03]And so then once this MOU is finalized,

[112:07]once we have the ceremonial signing on Friday

[112:10]and the war ends, the straight opens,

[112:13]then there's sort of like a phase two that begins.

[112:17]There's this 60 day period,

[112:19]where now we're gonna get down to business

[112:21]and figure out what we're gonna do about

[112:23]Iran centrifuges, which enrich uranium.

[112:27]They're already existing stockpile

[112:29]of highly enriched uranium.

[112:31]And then how we're going to incentivize them to do this by over time.

[112:37]unfreezing the money that has been frozen in foreign banks,

[112:41]which is Iranian money,

[112:43]and the lifting of primary and secondary sanctions,

[112:46]which had been applied

[112:47]because of Iran's nuclear program.

[112:49]So those are kind of the three big categories of the MOU.

[112:54]It is ending the war, it's opening up the straight,

[112:57]and then it's talking about the future conditions

[113:04]Now, the first thing to say about the MOU, which is really interesting, and this is really

[113:09]the headline and this is the obvious, is this basically confirms that the United States has

[114:11]have pushed inflation even higher and could have collapsed our economy.

[142:38]because one party of the agreement

[142:40]is lying to their own people to save face,

[142:43]I don't think it's Iran.

[142:45]It's probably the United States.

[142:48]But let's talk specifically about these discrepancies.

[142:51]I actually think that these don't matter too much.

[142:54]I think it is very likely that our side is lying.

[142:57]And I also don't think it matters at all.

[143:01]Probably the United States did allow money to be unfrozen and given to Iran.

[143:06]As a matter of fact, that has been reported by some sources that the United States authorized

[143:19]And I believe that.

[143:20]And I believe that the United States would just simply lie about that.

[143:24]And I also think it's true that Iran is going to control the straight because they effectively

[143:29]do anyway.

[143:31]And they're going to use that control to charge a toll or charge a fee at some point.

[143:36]And I don't believe the United States will re-enter a conflict with Iran to prevent

[143:40]this from happening.

[143:42]And I think it's very plausible the United States will try to smooth that over.

[143:46]Again, with this like semantic loophole and say, well, it's not really a toll. Actually,

[143:52]it's an insurance fee. It's an environmental fee. And I think they're perfectly willing

[143:56]to either lie that it's happening altogether. Or again, they're going to find some loophole

[144:03]to spin it. And they're going to say it's not a toll when really it is.

[144:08]Here is the biggest problem. The biggest problem is going to be the conflict in Lebanon.

[144:29]effectively ended

[144:31]two and a half months ago at the beginning of April,

[144:34]that original ceasefire, the terms were

[144:37]that we would stop bombing Iran

[144:39]as long as Iran opened up the straight

[144:42]and then negotiated on their nuclear program.

[144:47]in April is identical to this one

[144:50]because this is realistically the same thing.

[144:53]In April, Trump announced,

[144:55]we're gonna stop bombing Iran,

[144:56]they're gonna open the straight

[144:58]and then over the next two weeks,

[145:00]We're going to talk about nukes. This deal is virtually the same. We're saying there's a cease

[145:07]fire. Iran is going to open up the straight. We're going to end our blockade. And then over the next

[145:13]60 days, we're going to negotiate on the nuclear program. The only thing that has changed is we've

[145:19]actually given more.

[145:21]We've actually, in concrete terms, given financial commitments to put together this reconstruction

[145:27]fund, unfreezes money, potentially we've already unfrozen some of the money and given

[145:32]them cash.

[145:35]That's the only difference.

[145:36]But if you recall, in that original ceasefire in April, Iran never opened the straight.

[145:42]That's why we're where we are today.

[145:44]Iran never opened the straight going back to the beginning of the war and continuing even

[145:50]through the so-called ceasefire in April.

[145:53]And why did Iran not open the straight?

[145:57]Because just like this memorandum of understanding that ceasefire in April had all these discrepancies

[146:04]within 24 hours, just like this.

[146:08]The deal was announced on a Tuesday, and within hours, Iran and Pakistan, which brokered the

[146:48]They didn't add that after the fact.

[146:51]They're not lying to their own people to sell it to their population.

[146:55]No, both Pakistan and Iran said back in April, Washington agreed it's a ceasefire everywhere.

Topic 8: Part 6 (Lebanon's role & Israel's sabotage)

[147:04]And if there's not a ceasefire everywhere, then there's no deal.

[147:08]And that's exactly what happened.

[147:11]Iran and Pakistan insisted,

[147:13]if you can't get Israel to stop attacking Lebanon,

[147:17]if you can't get a ceasefire on every front,

[147:20]then you're in violation of the deal.

[147:22]And if you're in violation,

[147:24]then we're not holding up our end

[147:26]and we're keeping the straight closed.

[147:28]And that's what happened.

[147:30]As soon as the deal was announced, Iran closed the straight again and the straight has remained

[147:36]closed up until this day.

[147:38]Now, what does this tell us?

Topic 9: Part 7 (Iran's independence & power dynamics)

[157:56]we need is a very short-term ceasefire because we need economic relief. We are

[158:02]actually desperate to end the conflict because we need the oil to flow.

[158:07]We actually need the oil to go out because our strategic petroleum reserve is just about out and

[158:13]Once that oil stops being pumped prices are gonna go up fast

[158:18]They're already going up, inflation is already ticking back up, and this is a big problem

[158:24]for the U.S. economy.

[158:25]So we really need that oil.

[158:27]And that's why, by the way, we're giving all these concessions, we're getting nothing,

[158:33]we're giving everything, we're giving waivers for Iran to sell their oil without

[158:38]the sanctions that have been imposed, because we need the supply to get back to normal. We're bribing

[158:44]them and paying them to open up the straight, unilaterally ending our blockade, because we need

[158:49]the supply to go back to normal. So it is imperative for the United States to get a short-term agreement

[158:55]so we can get that immediate economic relief we can start to normalize.

[158:59]And it's not to say that we're not going to go back into Iran in the future. We just need a respite.

[159:06]We need to build more interceptors.

[159:08]We need to make them more cheaply.

[159:09]We need to build more drones.

[159:12]We need our military to regroup and recalibrate our strategy.

[159:17]And fundamentally, we just need more oil.

[159:39]They know Israel and the United States fundamentally want to destroy Iran's ability to enrich

[159:51]And they know that on a long enough timeline, we're going to come back and come back until

[159:57]our mission is accomplished because that's what we've been doing.

[160:07]And then it was killing Soleimani and then it was going to war against their proxies.

[160:26]against Iran's sovereignty or their airspace,

[166:54]animosity between the US and Israel.

[169:34]not have some great strategic imperative in toppling the Iranian government.

[169:46]We want balance.

[169:47]We want stability.

[169:57]It would involve ground operations, the biggest special forces raid in history, who even knows

[170:02]if it's possible at all.

[170:04]So, toppling Iran's government is not really something that is even an imperative for us.

[170:12]Maybe it's on the wish list, but it's not an imperative.

[170:20]You can achieve that without toppling the government.
